APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it addresses multimodal vision–language comprehension and captioning, introduces a new dual machine-comprehension task and a large-scale MCIC dataset derived from COCO, and proposes a concrete MC-IC decoy-generation algorithm combining paragraph-vector embeddings with surface similarity. The paper evaluates standard neural baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN), uses expected metrics (accuracy, ROUGE-L, CIDEr, BLEU), includes human evaluation, and leverages standard pretraining (Inception-v3), so methods and evaluation meet venue standards. The work targets the right audience and is positioned appropriately within the vision–language and NLP/ML communities. | B(5): This paper sits squarely in the vision–language multimodal area that is of strong interest to top NLP/ML venues: it proposes a new dual vision-language comprehension task, a principled MC-IC decoy-generation algorithm (paragraph-vector + simSURF/BLEU), and releases a large-scale dataset built from COCO (MCIC). It evaluates with standard metrics (accuracy, ROUGE-L, CIDEr, BLEU), uses sensible baselines and neural models (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) and pre-trained Inception-v3 features, and includes human evaluation — all aligning with expectations for top conferences. The problem formulation, audience, methods, and evaluation are appropriate and well positioned within the related work on multimodal understanding and captioning.

reviewer_a.justification: This is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it addresses multimodal vision–language comprehension and captioning, introduces a new dual machine-comprehension task and a large-scale MCIC dataset derived from COCO, and proposes a concrete MC-IC decoy-generation algorithm combining paragraph-vector embeddings with surface similarity. The paper evaluates standard neural baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN), uses expected metrics (accuracy, ROUGE-L, CIDEr, BLEU), includes human evaluation, and leverages standard pretraining (Inception-v3), so methods and evaluation meet venue standards. The work targets the right audience and is positioned appropriately within the vision–language and NLP/ML communities.

It evaluates with standard metrics (accuracy, ROUGE-L, CIDEr, BLEU), uses sensible baselines and neural models (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) and pre-trained Inception-v3 features, and includes human evaluation — all aligning with expectations for top conferences. The problem formulation, audience, methods, and evaluation are appropriate and well positioned within the related work on multimodal understanding and captioning.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well organized (introducing a new DMC task, describing the MC-IC decoy-generation algorithm, dataset construction from COCO, experiments with baselines and neural models, and human evaluation) and its main contributions are clearly stated early. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage thorough, and experimental details (e.g., hyperparameters, datasets, evaluation metrics) are provided, which supports reproducibility. Overall the presentation is strong, with only minor presentation issues implied (hence not rated exceptional), but the summary does not indicate major problems with notation or clarity. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from task definition to methods (MC-IC decoy algorithm) and experiments (dataset creation, human evaluation, and model baselines). The main contributions are stated early (new DMC task, MC-IC dataset, decoy generation algorithm, and models), and writing quality is noted as good, suggesting clarity and limited grammatical issues. Notation and methodological descriptions seem consistent and well-defined enough to reproduce key elements (hyperparameters reported), and the experimental presentation (human evaluation, accuracy and captioning metrics) appears informative, so only minor presentation polish would be needed.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important problem by formalizing a dual vision–language machine comprehension task and supplying a large-scale MCIC dataset built from COCO with a principled MC-IC decoy-generation algorithm (paragraph-vector + simSURF), which fills a gap beyond naive random decoys. The human evaluation establishing empirical upper bounds and the suite of baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) plus the observed comprehension–generation trade-off suggest practical implications for how multimodal models are evaluated and trained. These resources and findings are likely to be adopted and to open new work on robust decoy construction and joint comprehension-captioning objectives, though they are more evolutionary than transformative for the broader AI field. | B(4): The paper tackles an important evaluation gap in vision–language research by introducing a dual machine-comprehension (DMC) task and an extensible MC-IC decoy-generation algorithm (paragraph-vector + simSURF BLEU) and releasing a large-scale MCIC dataset derived from COCO. It provides human evaluation to establish an empirical upper bound and strong baselines (e.g., Vec2seq+FFNN) and empirical insights about multi-task training increasing correlation between comprehension accuracy and captioning performance (and the observed trade-off). These contributions create useful resources and experimental findings that are likely to spur follow-on work and new evaluation paradigms in multimodal research, so the impact is strong though not necessarily transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important problem by formalizing a dual vision–language machine comprehension task and supplying a large-scale MCIC dataset built from COCO with a principled MC-IC decoy-generation algorithm (paragraph-vector + simSURF), which fills a gap beyond naive random decoys. The human evaluation establishing empirical upper bounds and the suite of baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) plus the observed comprehension–generation trade-off suggest practical implications for how multimodal models are evaluated and trained. These resources and findings are likely to be adopted and to open new work on robust decoy construction and joint comprehension-captioning objectives, though they are more evolutionary than transformative for the broader AI field.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important evaluation gap in vision–language research by introducing a dual machine-comprehension (DMC) task and an extensible MC-IC decoy-generation algorithm (paragraph-vector + simSURF BLEU) and releasing a large-scale MCIC dataset derived from COCO. It provides human evaluation to establish an empirical upper bound and strong baselines (e.g., Vec2seq+FFNN) and empirical insights about multi-task training increasing correlation between comprehension accuracy and captioning performance (and the observed trade-off). These contributions create useful resources and experimental findings that are likely to spur follow-on work and new evaluation paradigms in multimodal research, so the impact is strong though not necessarily transformative.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes a concrete new task formulation (dual vision-language machine comprehension) and a practical dataset-generation algorithm (MC-IC) that combines paragraph-vector embeddings with BLEU-based surface similarity to produce challenging decoys, which is a useful contribution. However, the core methods largely recombine known components (PV embeddings, surface similarity metrics, and standard encoder-decoder / FFNN architectures) rather than introducing fundamentally new algorithms, so the novelty is moderate. The authors do clearly distinguish their dataset/decoy-generation procedure and present multi-task modeling (Vec2seq+FFNN) and human evaluation, which adds informative empirical value but would likely be seen by experts as an incremental advance rather than a major conceptual breakthrough. | B(3): The paper frames a new dual vision-language machine comprehension (DMC) task and produces a large-scale MCIC dataset from COCO with a principled decoy-generation algorithm (MC-IC), which is a clear contribution. However, the decoy construction relies on a straightforward combination of paragraph-vector embeddings and surface similarity (BLEU/simSURF), and the proposed models (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) and multi-task training are reasonable but incremental extensions of existing vision–language methods. The work therefore offers moderate novelty through task/dataset design and evaluation rather than fundamentally new algorithms or theory.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a concrete new task formulation (dual vision-language machine comprehension) and a practical dataset-generation algorithm (MC-IC) that combines paragraph-vector embeddings with BLEU-based surface similarity to produce challenging decoys, which is a useful contribution. However, the core methods largely recombine known components (PV embeddings, surface similarity metrics, and standard encoder-decoder / FFNN architectures) rather than introducing fundamentally new algorithms, so the novelty is moderate. The authors do clearly distinguish their dataset/decoy-generation procedure and present multi-task modeling (Vec2seq+FFNN) and human evaluation, which adds informative empirical value but would likely be seen by experts as an incremental advance rather than a major conceptual breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper frames a new dual vision-language machine comprehension (DMC) task and produces a large-scale MCIC dataset from COCO with a principled decoy-generation algorithm (MC-IC), which is a clear contribution. However, the decoy construction relies on a straightforward combination of paragraph-vector embeddings and surface similarity (BLEU/simSURF), and the proposed models (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) and multi-task training are reasonable but incremental extensions of existing vision–language methods. The work therefore offers moderate novelty through task/dataset design and evaluation rather than fundamentally new algorithms or theory.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims (new DMC task, MC-IC decoy-generation algorithm, large MCIC dataset, human upper-bound, and models showing multi-task correlation) are clearly stated and supported by experiments using reasonable baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, hybrid), appropriate metrics (accuracy, ROUGE-L, CIDEr, BLEU), and human evaluation; hyperparameters and pretraining (Inception-v3) are reported. There are no mathematical derivations to invalidate, and the experimental methodology appears sound overall. Minor technical concerns remain—e.g., no mention of statistical significance testing and the Vec2seq model lacks attention compared to stronger captioning baselines—which do not appear to undermine the main conclusions. | B(4): The paper's main claims (a DMC task, the MC‑IC decoy generation algorithm using paragraph‑vector + BLEU surface similarity, a large COCO‑derived dataset, human evaluation, and baseline/neural model results showing a comprehension–generation correlation) are stated and appear supported by experiments using COCO, pre‑trained Inception‑v3 features, and multiple model baselines. There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify, and the experimental setup includes reasonable baselines and multi‑task training analyses. Minor technical issues include lack of reported statistical significance testing and some modeling choices (e.g., no attention mechanism) that are acknowledged by the authors but do not overturn the main empirical findings.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims (new DMC task, MC-IC decoy-generation algorithm, large MCIC dataset, human upper-bound, and models showing multi-task correlation) are clearly stated and supported by experiments using reasonable baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, hybrid), appropriate metrics (accuracy, ROUGE-L, CIDEr, BLEU), and human evaluation; hyperparameters and pretraining (Inception-v3) are reported. There are no mathematical derivations to invalidate, and the experimental methodology appears sound overall. Minor technical concerns remain—e.g., no mention of statistical significance testing and the Vec2seq model lacks attention compared to stronger captioning baselines—which do not appear to undermine the main conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims (a DMC task, the MC‑IC decoy generation algorithm using paragraph‑vector + BLEU surface similarity, a large COCO‑derived dataset, human evaluation, and baseline/neural model results showing a comprehension–generation correlation) are stated and appear supported by experiments using COCO, pre‑trained Inception‑v3 features, and multiple model baselines. There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify, and the experimental setup includes reasonable baselines and multi‑task training analyses. Minor technical issues include lack of reported statistical significance testing and some modeling choices (e.g., no attention mechanism) that are acknowledged by the authors but do not overturn the main empirical findings.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides substantial experiments: a new MCIC dataset with a principled decoy-generation algorithm (MC-IC), human evaluation to set an empirical upper bound, multiple baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) and joint comprehension+generation analyses showing the λgen trade-off. Hyperparameters are reported and related work is described thoroughly; limitations such as the lack of attention in Vec2seq and the comprehension/generation trade-off are discussed. However, the summary does not indicate detailed ablations of the decoy-generation components or broader architectural comparisons (e.g., attention-based models) and code availability is not mentioned, so some reproducibility/analysis gaps remain. | B(4): The experiments are fairly comprehensive: the authors construct a large MCIC dataset with algorithmic decoy generation, provide human evaluation upper-bounds, evaluate multiple baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) and analyze multi-task effects on comprehension and captioning. Sensitivity analysis is present in the form of varying the generation loss weight (λgen) showing a trade-off, but it is unclear from the summary whether key ablations were done (e.g., isolating the contribution of paragraph-vector vs. surface-similarity in MC-IC decoy generation). Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and several limitations are discussed (naive decoys baseline, lack of attention in Vec2seq, the observed trade-off), while reproducibility is partially supported by reported hyperparameters though code release is not mentioned.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides substantial experiments: a new MCIC dataset with a principled decoy-generation algorithm (MC-IC), human evaluation to set an empirical upper bound, multiple baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) and joint comprehension+generation analyses showing the λgen trade-off. Hyperparameters are reported and related work is described thoroughly; limitations such as the lack of attention in Vec2seq and the comprehension/generation trade-off are discussed. However, the summary does not indicate detailed ablations of the decoy-generation components or broader architectural comparisons (e.g., attention-based models) and code availability is not mentioned, so some reproducibility/analysis gaps remain.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are fairly comprehensive: the authors construct a large MCIC dataset with algorithmic decoy generation, provide human evaluation upper-bounds, evaluate multiple baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) and analyze multi-task effects on comprehension and captioning. Sensitivity analysis is present in the form of varying the generation loss weight (λgen) showing a trade-off, but it is unclear from the summary whether key ablations were done (e.g., isolating the contribution of paragraph-vector vs. surface-similarity in MC-IC decoy generation). Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and several limitations are discussed (naive decoys baseline, lack of attention in Vec2seq, the observed trade-off), while reproducibility is partially supported by reported hyperparameters though code release is not mentioned.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates a range of reasonable baselines (Inception-v3 image features + paragraph-vector caption embeddings, FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) on the MCIC/COCO data using standard metrics (accuracy, ROUGE-L, CIDEr, BLEU), and uses consistent data/metrics so comparisons appear fair. The authors analyze differences (e.g., trade-offs from multi-task training, limitations such as lack of attention in Vec2seq) and provide thorough related-work coverage. However, the experimental set lacks stronger recent attention-based captioning/comprehension baselines (e.g., Show-Attend-and-Tell or other SOTA multimodal models) and it is not explicit that prior methods are acknowledged when they perform comparably or better, hence minor omissions. | B(4): The paper compares against a reasonable set of baselines (Inception-v3 image features + paragraph-vector embeddings, FFNN, Vec2seq, and Vec2seq+FFNN) and evaluates all methods on the same MCIC dataset with consistent metrics (accuracy, ROUGE-L, CIDEr), indicating fair comparisons. The authors analyze differences (e.g., multi-task gains, trade-offs between comprehension and generation) and acknowledge architectural limitations (their Vec2seq lacks attention compared to Show-Attend-and-Tell). However, while related work coverage is described as thorough, the evaluation omits some strong recent attention-based or retrieval-style baselines that would strengthen empirical comparisons, hence a minor omission.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates a range of reasonable baselines (Inception-v3 image features + paragraph-vector caption embeddings, FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) on the MCIC/COCO data using standard metrics (accuracy, ROUGE-L, CIDEr, BLEU), and uses consistent data/metrics so comparisons appear fair. The authors analyze differences (e.g., trade-offs from multi-task training, limitations such as lack of attention in Vec2seq) and provide thorough related-work coverage. However, the experimental set lacks stronger recent attention-based captioning/comprehension baselines (e.g., Show-Attend-and-Tell or other SOTA multimodal models) and it is not explicit that prior methods are acknowledged when they perform comparably or better, hence minor omissions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a reasonable set of baselines (Inception-v3 image features + paragraph-vector embeddings, FFNN, Vec2seq, and Vec2seq+FFNN) and evaluates all methods on the same MCIC dataset with consistent metrics (accuracy, ROUGE-L, CIDEr), indicating fair comparisons. The authors analyze differences (e.g., multi-task gains, trade-offs between comprehension and generation) and acknowledge architectural limitations (their Vec2seq lacks attention compared to Show-Attend-and-Tell). However, while related work coverage is described as thorough, the evaluation omits some strong recent attention-based or retrieval-style baselines that would strengthen empirical comparisons, hence a minor omission.

main_claims: Introduce a new dual vision-language machine comprehension (DMC) task where, given an image, a system must choose the best caption among several decoys.

Propose an effective and extensible algorithm to generate challenging decoy captions (MC-IC algorithm) using paragraph-vector embeddings and linguistic surface similarity, and apply it to produce a large-scale dataset (MCIC) based on COCO.
Provide human evaluation results on the MCIC dataset to establish an empirical performance upper-bound and analyze human agreement.

Present baseline and neural models (e.g., FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) and show that multi-task training (comprehension + caption generation) yields a positive correlation between comprehension accuracy and captioning performance.
_summary.methodology: Create difficult decoy caption sets for each image by combining paragraph-vector (PV) embedding similarity with linguistic surface similarity (simSURF computed as BLEU), apply this MC-IC algorithm to COCO to produce the MCIC dataset, and evaluate baselines and neural models (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) on the task using accuracy and captioning metrics.
baselines: Inception-v3 image features + paragraph-vector caption embeddings (random-projected)
Vec2Seq model trained for caption generation (used for classification via perplexity)
metrics: Accuracy (detection of true target among decoys)
limitations: Naive decoy selection (random captions) is overly simplistic; careful decoy generation is required to produce challenging instances.
The Vec2seq model used does not support location-wise attention (unlike Show-Attend-and-Tell).
There is an inherent trade-off observed between comprehension (detection) accuracy and generation performance when increasing the generation loss weight (λgen).
_summary.topic_area: Vision–Language (multimodal) machine comprehension and image captioning
